guide.title: Collaboration primitives
guide.summary: Use collaboration drafts, comments, and follow-up requests to keep subject-bound operational discussion and next steps inside the workspace where the work is happening.
body: Shared drafts keep unfinished comments or follow-up requests visible to other users before they are published.
Comments are lightweight discussion notes tied to one concrete record such as a vendor review.
Follow-up requests turn the next step into an explicit tracked item with status, priority, assignee, and due date.
Plugins can consume these primitives without inventing their own local comment or task model.

concepts.label: External collaboration hardening baseline
concepts.summary: Minimum deployment and runtime controls required when exposing collaboration portals on the Internet.
concepts.why_it_exists: External collaboration routes handle sensitive review data and must not rely on default hosting assumptions.
how_to_use: Enforce HTTPS-only access and protect deployment secrets.
Keep upload controls strict and block executable payload types.
Use runtime lifecycle enforcement for collaborator blocking and link expiry.
Treat reminder/digest/cleanup schedulers as operational improvements, not access-security controls.

relations.summary: Collaborator lifecycle controls are part of the hardening baseline.
External link issuance and access flows must follow the hardening baseline.

concepts.label: Collaboration external collaborator
concepts.summary: An object-scoped external participant identity used to group issued links and enforce lifecycle controls.
concepts.why_it_exists: It separates collaborator lifecycle state from individual links so one blocked collaborator cannot keep accessing through an older token.
how_to_use: Track one collaborator record per contact email within each shared object scope.
Move lifecycle state between active and blocked depending on risk or review decisions.
Use collaborator lifecycle control when you need to suspend access without deleting historical links and audit records.
Access enforcement is runtime-based on each portal request and does not require background CRON jobs.

relations.summary: A collaborator can own multiple issued links over time for the same scoped object.

concepts.label: Collaboration external link
concepts.summary: A revocable, expirable token link scoped to one subject record and owned by one plugin component.
concepts.why_it_exists: It provides a reusable external access primitive that any domain plugin can consume without creating custom link tables.
how_to_use: Issue links per concrete subject record and owner component.
Scope permissions explicitly so the external participant can only perform allowed actions.
Track delivery, access, expiry, and revocation lifecycle from one shared model.

relations.summary: Each external link belongs to one scoped collaborator identity.
External links can support comment or request workflows through domain-specific portals.
Domain plugins can map external link participation into follow-up request workflows.

concepts.label: Collaboration draft
concepts.summary: A shared unfinished collaboration record that can later be promoted into a published comment or follow-up request.
concepts.why_it_exists: It preserves continuity when one user starts the work but another user needs to finish or publish it later in the same workspace.
how_to_use: Save a draft when the note or follow-up request is not ready to publish yet.
Use shared drafts to keep unfinished work visible across reviewers instead of hiding it in personal notes.
Promote the draft to a comment or follow-up request when the content is ready to become part of the operational record.

relations.summary: A draft can become a published collaboration comment.
A draft can become a published follow-up request.

concepts.label: Collaboration comment
concepts.summary: A lightweight discussion note stored against one concrete subject record.
concepts.why_it_exists: It keeps operational context close to the record instead of scattering short discussion points across email or chat.
how_to_use: Add comments for clarifications, reviewer notes, decision rationale, or small coordination updates.
Mention functional actors when you want the note to create an explicit cue for the principals linked to those actors.
Use comments for discussion and context, not for structured follow-up work that needs an owner and due date.

relations.summary: Comments can explain why a follow-up request was created or updated.
Some comments start as shared drafts before they are published.

concepts.label: Collaboration request
concepts.summary: A tracked follow-up item tied to one concrete record, with priority, assignee, status, and optional due date.
concepts.why_it_exists: It moves the product from passive recordkeeping into explicit operational follow-up without forcing every plugin to build its own task model.
how_to_use: Create a request when the next step needs ownership or due date tracking.
Update status as work moves from open to in progress, waiting, done, or cancelled.
Use the handoff stage to show whether the request currently belongs to review, remediation, approval, or closed-loop follow-up.
Use mentioned actors when several governed roles should receive a visible cue even if only one actor owns the request.
Assign the request to a functional actor when accountability should stay anchored to a governed role rather than one login.

relations.summary: Comments can capture the reasoning or context around a request.
Some follow-up requests start as shared drafts before they are published.
